competitors.label: useTransition
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical React reference for transitions and non-urgent work, which is the most concrete official entry point into concurrent rendering behavior.
theirStrengths: It is the authoritative reference for how React marks work as non-urgent.
theirGaps: It is less direct on the interview misconception that concurrent rendering means parallel DOM work.

realWorldUseCases: urgent updates like typing stay responsive.
actionableExamples: React can interrupt rendering mid-way to handle urgent updates first, then continue rendering where it left off.
followUpConfusion: Concurrent rendering is not “React rendering in parallel.”

realWorldUseCases: The docs are authoritative on transitions, but our page is more explicit about the production problem being solved: jank from mixing expensive tree work with urgent input.
actionableExamples: The official examples are strong enough that the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is clearer on the likely follow-up that concurrency is scheduling and interruption, not parallel UI mutation.

competitors.winReason: The official docs are authoritative on transitions, but our page is stronger because it compresses concurrent rendering into the exact responsiveness and mental-model distinction interviewers usually want.
